_comment: Fixed prompt set for hermes-metal benchmarks. Lengths are approximate input-token counts (model-specific tokenization will vary by ~5%). decode_tokens is max_new_tokens. Same temperature/seed across backends for fairness.

prompts.text: Briefly explain what unified memory architecture means on Apple Silicon, and why it matters for running LLM inference locally.

Summarize the following passage in three sentences.

The Apollo program was the third United States human spaceflight program carried out by NASA, which succeeded in preparing and landing the first humans on the Moon from 1968 to 1972. First conceived in 1960 during President Eisenhower's administration as a three-person spacecraft to follow the one-person Project Mercury, Apollo was later dedicated to President John F. Kennedy's national goal for the 1960s of landing a man on the Moon and returning him safely to the Earth in an address to Congress on May 25, 1961. It was the third US human spaceflight program to fly, preceded by the two-person Project Gemini conceived in 1961 to extend spaceflight capability in support of Apollo. Kennedy's goal was accomplished on the Apollo 11 mission when astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin landed their Apollo Lunar Module on the Moon on July 20, 1969, and walked on its surface while Michael Collins remained in lunar orbit in the command and service module, and all three landed safely on Earth on July 24. Five subsequent Apollo missions also landed astronauts on the Moon, the last in December 1972, in which 12 men walked on the Moon. These six Apollo spaceflights are the only times humans have landed on another celestial body. Apollo ran from 1961 to 1972, with the first crewed flight in 1968. It encountered a major setback in 1967 when an Apollo 1 cabin fire killed the entire crew during a prelaunch test. After the first successful landing, sufficient flight hardware remained for nine follow-on landings with a plan for extended lunar geological and astrophysical exploration. Budget cuts forced the cancellation of three of these. Five of the remaining six missions achieved successful landings, but the Apollo 13 landing was prevented by an oxygen tank explosion in transit to the Moon, crippling the CSM. The crew returned to Earth safely by using the Lunar Module as a lifeboat for these functions.

Below is an excerpt from a software engineering text. Identify the three most important architectural ideas in it and explain how they interact. Repeat each section internally for emphasis as needed.

=== BEGIN EXCERPT ===
A well-designed software system separates concerns along boundaries that match the problem domain rather than the implementation technology. The single most reliable predictor of long-term maintainability is whether a module's public interface describes WHAT it does, not HOW. Implementations are free to evolve under stable contracts; contracts that leak implementation detail bind every consumer to the next refactor. The corollary is that the seams between modules should be drawn where the rate of change is lowest, not where the codebase happens to feel symmetrical. Symmetry is an aesthetic; rate of change is a measurement.

State is the second axis. A pure function of its inputs is trivially testable, parallelizable, and cacheable; a function that touches global state is none of those. The discipline of pushing state to the edges ("functional core, imperative shell") buys you a large unit-testable region in the middle of the program. Where state is unavoidable, prefer immutable updates that produce new values over in-place mutation that destroys old ones; the cost in allocations is almost always recovered by the cost saved in debugging concurrency bugs that no longer exist. Persistent data structures are the textbook tool here, but even a discipline of `dataclasses.replace()` or struct-spread is sufficient in most application code.

The third axis is failure. Distributed systems are the canonical example, but every program above a certain complexity is a distributed system in disguise: cache layers, file systems, kernel I/O queues, and even other threads in the same process all introduce partial-failure modes. The dominant question is not 'how do I prevent failure' but 'how do I make failure observable and recoverable'. Observability requires that the program emit enough signal — structured logs, metrics, traces — that an operator can reconstruct what happened without re-running the program. Recoverability requires that operations be either idempotent or transactional; in practice, idempotency is far cheaper to engineer and composes better with retries than two-phase commit.

These three axes — interface design, state discipline, and failure model — are not independent. A clean interface that exposes mutable state is a footgun. A pure functional core that cannot be observed is a black box. A failure-tolerant outer shell that wraps a stateful inner core inherits the inner core's race conditions. The art of system design is choosing which compromises to make on which axis, given the actual workload and team. There is no universally correct answer; there are only locally appropriate ones, and the discipline of revisiting them as the workload changes.

The practical advice that follows from these axes is unglamorous. Write down the contract before the implementation. Make state changes explicit at the function boundary. Treat every external interaction as fallible. Keep the testable region as large as possible, and test it ruthlessly. Push policy decisions out toward configuration; keep mechanism in code. When you encounter a part of the system that resists testing, treat the difficulty as a design signal, not a testing problem. The hardest part of software engineering is not writing code that works; it is writing code that continues to work as the world around it changes, and the three axes above are the structural support for that continuity.
=== END EXCERPT ===
